stem: A restaurant offers 3 appetizers and 4 main courses. How many different appetizer-and-main-course meals are possible?
solutionStandard: By the fundamental counting principle: \(3 \times 4 = 12\).

stem: In how many ways can 4 people be arranged in a line?
solutionStandard: \(4! = 4 \times 3 \times 2 \times 1 = 24\).

stem: A password consists of 3 different letters chosen from A, B, C, D, E, arranged in order. How many such passwords are possible?
solutionStandard: Order matters and letters can't repeat: \(5 \times 4 \times 3 = 60\).

stem: In how many ways can a president and a vice president be chosen from a group of 6 people (no one can hold both offices)?
solutionStandard: 6 choices for president, then 5 remaining for vice president: \(6 \times 5 = 30\).

stem: Compare the two quantities.
quantityA: The number of ways to arrange 4 distinct books on a shelf
solutionStandard: \(4! = 24\). Since \(24 > 20\), Quantity A is greater.

stem: A license plate consists of 2 letters followed by 3 digits. Letters and digits may repeat. How many license plates are possible?
solutionStandard: Each of the 2 letter positions has 26 choices, and each of the 3 digit positions has 10 choices: \(26 \times 26 \times 10 \times 10 \times 10 = 26^2 \times 10^3\).

stem: In how many ways can 1st, 2nd, and 3rd place be awarded among 7 runners (no ties)?
Which of the following equal \(5!\)? Select all that apply.
In how many distinct ways can the letters of the word "LEVEL" be arranged?
solutionStandard: "LEVEL" has 5 letters with L repeated twice and E repeated twice: \(\frac{5!}{2! \times 2!} = \frac{120}{4} = 30\).

stem: 5 people, including a couple who insist on sitting next to each other, are seated in a row. How many seating arrangements are possible?
solutionStandard: Treat the couple as a single block: 4 units to arrange in \(4! = 24\) ways, and within the block the couple can switch places in \(2! = 2\) ways. Total: \(24 \times 2 = 48\).

stem: A restaurant offers {{a}} appetizers and {{b}} main courses. How many different appetizer-and-main-course meals are possible?
solutionStandard: By the fundamental counting principle: \({{a}} \times {{b}} = {{a*b}}\).

stem: In how many ways can {{n}} people be arranged in a line?
solutionStandard: The number of arrangements of {{n}} people is \(n! = {{n}}!\).

stem: A password consists of {{k}} different letters chosen from a set of {{pool}} distinct letters, arranged in order. How many such passwords are possible?
solutionStandard: Order matters and letters can't repeat, so this is \(P({{pool}},{{k}})\): multiply {{k}} descending values starting from {{pool}}.

stem: In how many ways can a president and a vice president be chosen from a group of {{n}} people (no one can hold both offices)?
solutionStandard: {{n}} choices for president, then {{n-1}} remaining for vice president: \({{n}} \times {{n-1}} = {{n*(n-1)}}\).

stem: Compare the two quantities.
quantityA: The number of ways to arrange {{n}} distinct books on a shelf
solutionStandard: Quantity A is \(n!\), which grows far faster than the linear quantity \(3n\); already at \(n=4\), \(4!=24>12=3(4)\), and the gap only widens as \(n\) increases. Quantity A is always greater.

stem: A code consists of 3 letters followed by 2 digits. Letters and digits may repeat. How many codes are possible?
solutionStandard: Each of the 3 letter positions has 26 choices, and each of the 2 digit positions has 10 choices: \(26 \times 26 \times 26 \times 10 \times 10 = 26^3 \times 10^2\).

stem: In how many ways can 1st, 2nd, ..., and {{k}}th place be awarded among {{n}} contestants (no ties)?
solutionStandard: This is \(P({{n}},{{k}}) = \dfrac{ {{n}}! }{ ({{n}}-{{k}})! }\): multiply {{k}} descending values starting from {{n}}.

stem: Which of the following equal \(P(7,3)\)? Select all that apply.
solutionStandard: \(P(7,3) = 7\times6\times5 = 210\), and \(\dfrac{7!}{4!}\) is exactly the same computation. \(7\times6\times5\times4=840\) is \(P(7,4)\), not \(P(7,3)\).

stem: In how many distinct ways can the letters of the word "TOMATO" be arranged?
solutionStandard: "TOMATO" has 6 letters with T repeated twice and O repeated twice: \(\dfrac{6!}{2! \times 2!} = \dfrac{720}{4} = 180\).

stem: {{n}} people, including a couple who insist on sitting next to each other, are seated in a row. How many seating arrangements are possible?
solutionStandard: Treat the couple as a single block: {{n-1}} units to arrange in \(({{n}}-1)!\) ways, and within the block the couple can switch places in \(2!=2\) ways.
